<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six environmental impacts of the sugar industry.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. High BOD and COD wastewater discharge polluting surface water bodies.
2. Emission of particulate matter from bagasse-fired boilers causing air pollution.
3. Generation of large quantities of solid waste including bagasse ash, pressmud, and trash.
4. Noise pollution from crushers, mills, and turbines.
5. Greenhouse gas emissions (CO₂, CH₄) from anaerobic effluent ponds.
6. Soil and groundwater contamination from molasses spillage and unsanitary landfills.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the COD/BOD ratio in assessing effluent treatability?**

**Model Answer:**

The COD/BOD ratio is a **biodegradability index** used to evaluate how amenable a wastewater is to biological treatment.

- **Ratio < 2.5:** Wastewater is easily biodegradable → suitable for conventional biological treatment.
- **Ratio 2.5 – 4.0:** Moderately biodegradable → may need pre-treatment or extended aeration.
- **Ratio > 4.0:** Poorly biodegradable → physico-chemical or advanced oxidation processes required.

For sugar industry effluent, the typical COD/BOD ratio is **2.0 – 2.5**, indicating it is highly amenable to anaerobic-aerobic biological treatment.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **environmental aspects and impacts** of the sugar industry across the three environmental media (water, air, land). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **sequence of treatment units** for a sugar mill effluent plant producing 2,000 m³/day with BOD₅ = 4,000 mg/L to comply with CPCB discharge norms. Show all design calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) (7 marks):

**1. Water Pollution (2.5 marks)**
- Sources: Cane wash water, condenser cooling water, spillages, and floor washings.
- Characteristics: High BOD (3,000–6,000 mg/L), high COD (6,000–10,000 mg/L), acidic pH (4–5), high temperature (45–55°C), dark brown color due to melanoidin pigments.
- Impact: Eutrophication, fish kills, oxygen depletion in receiving water bodies.

**2. Air Pollution (2.5 marks)**
- Source: Bagasse-fired boilers.
- Pollutants: Particulate matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅), SO₂ from sulphur content of bagasse, NOₓ from high combustion temperatures, CO from incomplete combustion.
- Impact: Respiratory diseases in nearby communities, smog formation, acid rain.

**3. Solid Waste / Soil Pollution (2 marks)**
- Bagasse ash: 6–8 kg per tonne of cane; contains silica, potassium, calcium.
- Pressmud: 30–50 kg per tonne of cane; rich in organic matter and used as compost.
- Molasses: 40–50 kg per tonne of cane; highly polluting if spilled.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) (7 marks):

**Step 1: Influent Characteristics (0.5 marks)**
$$Q = 2{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}, \quad \text{BOD}_5 = 4{,}000 \text{ mg/L}, \quad k = 0.30 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Ultimate BOD (1 mark)**

$$L_0 = \dfrac{4{,}000}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.30}} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{1 - e^{-1.5}}$$

$$L_0 = \dfrac{4{,}000}{1 - 0.2231} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{0.7769} = 5{,}149.2 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Total BOD Load (0.5 marks)**

$$\text{BOD Load} = Q \times L_0 = 2{,}000 \times 5{,}149.2 \times 10^{-3} = 10{,}298.4 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4: Choose Treatment Sequence (3 marks)**

1. **Screening and Grit Removal** – removes large solids.
2. **Equalization Tank** – smooths flow and load fluctuations; volume = $2{,}000 \times 0.25 = 500$ m³.
3. **Neutralization Tank** – adjusts pH to 6.5–7.5.
4. **UASB Reactor** – anaerobic digestion; volume ≈ $Q \times \text{HRT} = 2{,}000 \times 1.0 = 2{,}000$ m³; removal efficiency 70%.
5. **Activated Sludge Unit** – aerobic polishing; volume using F/M = 0.25 day⁻¹:

$$V = \dfrac{Q \times S_{\text{in}}}{F/M \times X} = \dfrac{2{,}000 \times 1{,}544.7 \times 10^{-3}}{0.25 \times 3{,}000} = 4.12 \text{ m}^3$$

(Note: $S_{\text{in}}$ after UASB = $5{,}149.2 \times 0.30 = 1{,}544.8$ mg/L). Scale up with safety factor → design volume = **50–80 m³**.

6. **Secondary Clarifier** – solids separation.
7. **Tertiary Polishing via Constructed Wetland** – removes residual color and BOD.
8. **Sludge Thickening and Dewatering** – sludge management.

**Step 5: Final Effluent BOD₅ (1.5 marks)**

After UASB: $1{,}544.8$ mg/L  
After ASP (90%): $1{,}544.8 \times 0.10 = 154.5$ mg/L  
After Wetland (50%): $154.5 \times 0.50 = 77.2$ mg/L  

$$L_{\text{final}} = 77.2 \text{ mg/L} < 100 \text{ mg/L} \Rightarrow \textbf{COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 6: Sludge and Biogas Recovery (0.5 marks)**

- Biogas generation ≈ 0.4 m³ per kg BOD removed.
- Recovered biogas can supplement boiler fuel, reducing fossil fuel consumption.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **Environmental Management System (EMS) framework as per ISO 14001:2015** and explain how it can be applied in a sugar mill. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **methods for controlling air pollution** from a sugar industry boiler with specific reference to particulate matter and gaseous pollutants. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) (7 marks):

**ISO 14001:2015 EMS Framework (3.5 marks):**

The standard follows the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle** with **10 clauses**:

- **Context of the Organization (Cl. 4):** Identify internal/external issues, needs of interested parties.
- **Leadership (Cl. 5):** Top management commitment, environmental policy.
- **Planning (Cl. 6):** Identify environmental aspects, legal requirements, risks/opportunities, objectives.
- **Support (Cl. 7):** Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information.
- **Operation (Cl. 8):** Operational planning and control, emergency preparedness.
- **Performance Evaluation (Cl. 9):** Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review.
- **Improvement (Cl. 10):** Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement.

**Application to Sugar Mill (3.5 marks):**

1. **Environmental Aspects:** Identify wastewater, emissions, solid waste, noise.
2. **Legal Register:** Maintain CPCB norms, Water Act, Air Act, Hazardous Waste Rules.
3. **Objectives:** Reduce water consumption by 20%, achieve zero discharge, recycle 80% ash.
4. **Operational Controls:** SOPs for ETP operation, bagasse storage, emergency response for molasses spillage.
5. **Monitoring:** Daily BOD/COD testing, stack PM monitoring, noise surveys.
6. **Audit & Review:** Quarterly internal audits, annual management review.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) (7 marks):

**Particulate Matter Control (3.5 marks):**

1. **Cyclone Separators:** Pre-cleaning of flue gas; efficiency 70–90% for coarse particles.
2. **Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP):** Efficiency > 99% for fine PM; working principle based on charging particles and collecting on plates.
3. **Baghouse Fabric Filters:** Efficiency 99.9%; works for fine and sticky particles.
4. **Wet Scrubbers:** Venturi scrubbers for sticky/molasses-laden particulates.

**Gaseous Pollutant Control (3.5 marks):**

1. **SO₂ Control:**
   - Use low-sulphur bagasse.
   - Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD) with limestone/lime slurry.
   - Dry sorbent injection.

2. **NOₓ Control:**
   - Low-NOx burners with staged air/fuel mixing.
   - Flue gas recirculation (FGR).
   - Selective catalytic reduction (SCR).

3. **CO₂ Mitigation:**
   - Energy efficiency improvements (high-pressure boilers, cogeneration).
   - Carbon capture and storage (emerging technology).
   - Compensate via afforestation and renewable energy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Writing generic EMS definitions without **specific application** to the sugar industry → loses 2–3 marks in Part (a).
> 2. Forgetting to include **legal/regulatory references** (e.g., CPCB norms, Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981) → loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. Skipping numerical units and presenting only equations → loses marks for "completeness of solution."
> 4. Mixing up **BOD₅** with **BOD ultimate (L₀)** in design calculations → entire numerical becomes wrong.
> 5. Omitting **sludge management** and **biogas recovery** from the ETP design → treated as incomplete design → 1–2 marks deducted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Sugar industry is a **multi-media polluting industry** affecting water, air, and soil.
- [ ] Typical cane crushing produces **10–11% sugar, 4–5% molasses, 3–5% pressmud, 25–30% bagasse** by weight.
- [ ] **BOD₅ of raw effluent** ranges from **3,000 to 6,000 mg/L**; CPCB standard for discharge is **100 mg/L**.
- [ ] **COD/BOD ratio** is the standard **biodegradability index** (typical sugar value: 2.0–2.5).
- [ ] Standard treatment train: **Screening → Equalization → UASB → ASP → Clarifier → Wetland**.
- [ ] **Biogas generation** from spent wash is a key **circular economy / cleaner production** opportunity.
- [ ] **Bagasse is a renewable fuel** — sugar mills are often **net exporters of electricity** via cogeneration.
- [ ] **Particulate control** devices: Cyclone (pre-cleaning) → ESP/Bag Filter (final control).
- [ ] **ISO 14001:2015** uses the **PDCA cycle** with **10 clauses** (Clauses 4–10).
- [ ] Always include **legal compliance (CPCB/SPCB norms)** in any EMS-related answer.
- [ ] For numerical problems, always carry **units** through every step and quote the final answer with **units**.
- [ ] Sugar industry is a classic **case study** for **Cleaner Production (CP)** and **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** concepts.
- [ ] The **F/M ratio** is critical for biological reactor design (typical value 0.2–0.5 day⁻¹ for conventional ASP).
- [ ] Remember the formula: $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ — used in almost every treatment efficiency problem.
- [ ] **Anaerobic filters / UASB** are preferred for sugar industry due to high strength effluent and biogas potential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulp & Paper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulp & Paper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is the Pulp and Paper Industry?

The **Pulp and Paper Industry** is a global manufacturing sector that converts lignocellulosic raw materials (wood, bamboo, agricultural residues like bagasse and straw, and recycled paper) into **pulp** (a fibrous slurry) and subsequently into **paper, paperboard, and related cellulose-based products**. It is classified as one of the **17 most polluting industrial sectors** by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** of India and is a major focus area under **Environmental Management Systems (EMS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *The Pulp and Paper industry is a process-intensive, resource-heavy manufacturing system that integrates forestry, chemical engineering, and water-energy nexus operations. It is characterized by high water consumption (75–250 m³/tonne of paper), significant chemical usage, and generation of complex wastewater streams containing chlorinated organics, lignin derivatives, and high BOD/COD loads.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Spaghetti Maker" 🍝

Imagine making paper the way you would cook spaghetti:
- **Raw material (wood chips)** = dry spaghetti sticks (rigid, bound together).
- **Pulping** = boiling the spaghetti until the starch glue dissolves and the individual noodle strands separate in water.
- **Bleaching** = rinsing the noodles until the water runs clear (removing the brown glue-residue).
- **Paper making** = spreading the wet noodle strands on a flat tray, pressing them together, and drying — they interlock into a sheet.
- **Environmental pollution** = the dirty, brown, smelly water you pour down the drain.

Just as you would want to filter and reuse that water in a smart kitchen, an **EMS in a paper mill** controls, recycles, and treats every stream to minimize pollution and resource loss.

### 1.2 Key Industry Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Water consumption** | **75 – 250** | m³ / tonne of paper |
| **Energy consumption** | **20 – 30** | GJ / tonne |
| **COD of raw effluent** | **1,000 – 10,000** | mg/L |
| **BOD of raw effluent** | **300 – 4,000** | mg/L |
| **Bleach plant effluent color** | **1,000 – 5,000** | Pt-Co units |
| **Solid waste (sludge)** | **50 – 150** | kg / tonne |

> [!NOTE]
> The pulp and paper industry consumes approximately **40% of all industrial wood traded globally** and accounts for nearly **5% of global industrial energy consumption** — making it a prime candidate for EMS implementation under **ISO 14001:2015**.

### 1.3 Major Raw Materials Used

- **Wood** (Softwood: Pine, Spruce; Hardwood: Eucalyptus, Acacia) — primary source.
- **Agricultural residues** — Bagasse (sugarcane), Rice straw, Wheat straw, Bamboo.
- **Recycled fibre** — Old Corrugated Containers (OCC), Mixed Office Waste (MOW), De-inked pulp (DIP).
- **Non-fibrous additives** — Fillers (CaCO₃, TiO₂), Sizing agents (rosin, AKD), Dyes, Optical brighteners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Process Flow of an Integrated Pulp & Paper Mill

The manufacturing chain is divided into **two major blocks**:

### A. Pulping Block (Fibre Production)

1. **Wood Yard / Raw Material Preparation**
   - Debarking, chipping, screening.
2. **Pulping** (fibre separation from lignin) — *Three main methods*:
   - **Mechanical Pulping** (Stone Groundwood, Refiner Mechanical Pulp — RMP, Thermo-Mechanical Pulp — TMP).
   - **Chemical Pulping** (Kraft/Sulphate process, Sulphite process).
   - **Semi-chemical pulping** (Neutral Sulphite Semi-Chemical — NSSC).
3. **Brown Stock Washing** — removes dissolved lignin and spent chemicals.
4. **Screening & Cleaning** — removes uncooked chips (rejects), sand, plastics.

### B. Bleaching & Paper Machine Block

5. **Bleaching** — multi-stage sequences (e.g., CEH, CEDED, OD₀EopD₁, ECF, TCF).
6. **Stock Preparation** — beating/refining, additive mixing.
7. **Paper Machine** — Fourdrinier or Twin-wire former → Press section → Drying section → Calendering → Reeling.
8. **Finishing** — slitting, sheeting, packaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Kraft (Sulphate) process dominates ~80% of global chemical pulp production** because it produces the strongest fibres, tolerates diverse wood species, and enables efficient chemical recovery via the **Tomlinson Recovery Boiler**.

### 2.2 The Kraft Pulping Reaction (Core Chemistry)

The active chemicals in Kraft pulping are **White Liquor** containing:

$$\text{NaOH} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}$$

The key delignification reaction with lignin in wood:

$$\text{Lignin–O–R} + \text{NaOH} + \text{Na}_2\text{S} \longrightarrow \text{Na–Lignin fragments} + \text{Na–R–OH} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$

The dissolved lignin and spent chemicals form **Black Liquor** — a critical stream from both pollution and resource-recovery perspectives.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sulfidity Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Na}_2\text{S}}{\text{NaOH} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}} \times 100$ | % (Optimum: 25–35%) |
| 2 | **Active Alkali (AA)** | $\text{NaOH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}$ (as Na₂O) | g/L or % on oven-dry wood |
| 3 | **Kraft Yield** | $\dfrac{\text{Oven-dry pulp mass}}{\text{Oven-dry wood mass}} \times 100$ | % (Typically 45–55%) |
| 4 | **BOD Load** | $\text{BOD}_5 = \text{BOD}_{\text{ult}} \times (1 - e^{-kt})$ | mg/L, k=0.23/day⁻¹ |
| 5 | **Rejection Ratio (RO)** | $\left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$ | % — for membrane treatment |
| 6 | **Pulp Consistency** | $\dfrac{\text{Oven-dry fibre mass}}{\text{Total slurry mass}} \times 100$ | % (Brown stock: 3–5%; High-density: 25–35%) |
| 7 | **Recovery Boiler Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{steam out}}}{Q_{\text{fuel in}}} \times 100$ | % (Modern boilers: 85–90%) |
| 8 | **AOX Load** | Adsorbable Organic Halides (Cl-bound organics) | kg Cl / tonne pulp |
| 9 | **H-Factor** | $H = \int_{0}^{t} \exp\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right) dt$ | dimensionless pulping kinetics |
| 10 | **COD/BOD Ratio** | $\text{COD} / \text{BOD}_5$ | Indicates biodegradability (paper mill ≈ 2–3) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **H-Factor** (developed by Vroom in 1957) is universally used in Kraft mills to model the combined effect of **time** and **temperature** on delignification — making it the heart of digester control.

## 2.4 Environmental Significance of Each Stream

- **Air emissions**: **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂)**, **Nitrogen oxides (NOₓ)**, **Hydrogen sulphide (H₂S)**, **Total Reduced Sulphur (TRS)** compounds, **particulate matter**, and **CO₂**.
- **Liquid effluents**: High BOD/COD, dark color (from lignin chromophores), **AOX**, **chlorinated dioxins and furans** (in chlorine-bleached mills).
- **Solid waste**: **Bark**, **ash**, **grit**, **dregs**, **green liquor sludge**, **biological sludge** from effluent treatment.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering

These parameters are not merely academic — they are embedded in:
- **CPCB ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge)** compliance calculations.
- **ISO 14001:2015** Aspect-Impact registers for paper mills.
- **Carbon footprint accounting** under GHG Protocol Scope 1, 2, 3.
- **EIA notifications** under the **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification, 2006** (India) for new paper mills.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Kraft Pulping H-Factor

The delignification of wood during Kraft cooking is governed by an **Arrhenius-type first-order rate equation**. We now derive the H-Factor used in industrial digester control.

**Step 1: Rate Equation for Lignin Removal**

The rate of lignin dissolution follows a first-order dependency on temperature:

$$-\dfrac{dL}{dt} = k \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the residual lignin fraction and $k$ is the rate constant.

**Step 2: Arrhenius Temperature Dependence**

The rate constant $k$ varies with absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin) as:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/mol·K**
- $T$ = temperature in K

**Step 3: Reference Temperature Normalization**

Vroom normalized the equation to a reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$ and introduced a relative rate $k_r$:

$$k_r = \dfrac{k}{k_{\text{ref}}} = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{ref}}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4: Empirical Simplification (Hartler's Constants)**

Using the experimentally determined reference values — $T_{\text{ref}} = 373.15$ K (100 °C) and $E_a/R = 16{,}113$ K — the equation reduces to the familiar Vroom form:

$$k_r = \exp\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right)$$

**Step 5: Integration Over Cooking Time**

The total cook is expressed as the time-integrated relative rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \int_{0}^{t_{\text{cook}}} k_r \, dt \\[6pt]
  &= \int_{0}^{t_{\text{cook}}} \exp\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T(t)}\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Practical Application**

- A mill maintaining **H = 1,000** at 170 °C cooks for approximately **2.5 hours**.
- A change in digester temperature profile by ±5 °C can change $H$ by 25–40% — directly affecting pulp quality (Kappa number) and bleachability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reasoning behind the math**: The H-Factor allows operators to *trade time for temperature*. A mill can shorten the cook (save energy) by raising temperature, or extend the cook (avoid fibre damage) by lowering temperature — while delivering the same degree of delignification.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Sulphidity Calculation

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
A Kraft mill's white liquor analysis shows: NaOH = 80 g/L (as Na₂O), Na₂S = 25 g/L (as Na₂O). Calculate the **Active Alkali** and the **Sulphidity** in %.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify values**

$$[\text{NaOH}] = 80 \text{ g/L (as Na}_2\text{O)}$$

$$[\text{Na}_2\text{S}] = 25 \text{ g/L (as Na}_2\text{O)}$$

**Step 2: Compute Active Alkali (AA)**

$$
\text{AA} = \text{NaOH} + \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AA} &= 80 + \dfrac{25}{2} \\[4pt]
         &= 80 + 12.5 \\[4pt]
         &= 92.5 \text{ g/L (as Na}_2\text{O)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Sulphidity**

$$
\text{Sulphidity (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Na}_2\text{S}}{\text{NaOH} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}} \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sulphidity} &= \dfrac{25}{80 + 25} \times 100 \\[4pt]
                  &= \dfrac{25}{105} \times 100 \\[4pt]
                  &= 23.81 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation**

A sulphidity of **23.8%** lies slightly **below the optimal range (25–35%)** — this would slow down delignification and increase chemical consumption. The mill should raise Na₂S dosing by approximately 5 g/L to reach 28.6% sulphidity.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Pulping Processes

| Feature | Mechanical | Sulphite | **Kraft (Sulphate)** | NSSC (Semi-Chemical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Yield (%)** | 90–95 | 45–55 | **45–55** | 70–85 |
| **Fibre strength** | Low | Medium | **Highest** | Medium |
| **Chemical recovery** | None | Partial | **Full (Black liquor)** | Partial |
| **Environmental load** | High energy | High SO₂ | **Moderate (manageable)** | Medium |
| **Bleachability** | Poor (lignin retained) | Moderate | **Good** | Moderate |
| **Wood tolerance** | Limited | Limited | **Excellent (all species)** | Moderate |
| **Pulp Kappa number** | 80–120 | 30–50 | **20–35** | 50–80 |
| **Dominant use** | Newsprint | Tissue, printing | **Packaging, printing, specialty** | Corrugating medium |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip**: The Kraft process wins on **fibre strength** and **chemical recovery**, which is why ~80% of mills worldwide (and virtually all large Indian mills) use it.

## 3.4 Environmental Load Calculation Example

**Problem:**
A paper mill produces **200 tonnes/day** of bleached pulp. The effluent has a **BOD₅ of 1,800 mg/L** and a flow of **40 m³/tonne** of pulp. Compute the **daily BOD load** in kg/day.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Total daily effluent volume**

$$Q = 200 \text{ t/d} \times 40 \text{ m}^3/\text{t} = 8{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

**Step 2: Convert BOD concentration to load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BOD Load} &= Q \times \text{BOD}_5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ (kg/mg conversion)} \\[4pt]
                &= 8{,}000 \times 1{,}800 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
                &= 14{,}400 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Express per tonne of pulp**

$$\text{Specific BOD Load} = \dfrac{14{,}400}{200} = 72 \text{ kg BOD/tonne pulp}$$

**Step 4: Compliance Check (CPCB Norms)**

The **CPCB standard** for bleached pulp mills is **≤ 25 kg BOD/tonne** (after treatment). Hence, the mill requires a treatment efficiency of:

$$
\eta_{\text{BOD}} = \dfrac{72 - 25}{72} \times 100 = 65.3\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU Valuation Warning):** Students often forget to multiply the BOD concentration by $10^{-3}$ to convert mg/L to kg/m³. This single omission leads to a **2–3 mark loss** in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Pulp & Paper Mill with EMS Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Wood Yard] --> A2[Chipper]
    A2 --> A3[Screener]
    A3 --> B1[Kraft Digester]
    B1 --> B2[Brown Stock Washer]
    B2 --> B3[Screens and Cleaners]
    B3 --> C1[Oxygen Delignification]
    C1 --> C2[Bleach Plant ECF TCF]
    C2 --> D1[Stock Preparation]
    D1 --> D2[Paper Machine Fourdrinier]
    D2 --> D3[Reeler and Finishing]
    E1[White Liquor Preparation] --> B1
    B1 -- Black Liquor --> F1[Evaporator]
    F1 --> F2[Tomlinson Recovery Boiler]
    F2 -- Green Liquor --> E1
    F2 --> G1[Power Generation]
    D2 -- Wastewater --> H1[Primary Clarifier]
    H1 --> H2[Aerated Lagoon]
    H2 --> H3[Activated Sludge]
    H3 --> I1[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    B2 -- Spent Liquor --> F1
    C2 -- Bleach Effluent --> H1
    H1 -- Sludge --> J1[Sludge Dewatering]
    J1 --> J2[Landfill or Boiler Co-firing]
    D2 -- Rejects --> K1[Recycled Fibre Plant]
    K1 --> D1
    A1 -- Bark and Dust --> F2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The flow follows two parallel loops — a **fibre loop** (top, A→D) producing paper, and a **chemical/water loop** (bottom, E→H→I) recovering and treating spent streams. EMS overlays (KPIs, monitoring points) sit on top of this physical layout.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — EMS Framework Applied to a Paper Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Top Management Commitment] --> P2[Environmental Policy]
    P2 --> P3[Planning Aspect Impact Register]
    P3 --> P4[Legal and Other Requirements]
    P4 --> P5[Objectives Targets Programs]
    P5 --> P6[Implementation Resources Roles]
    P6 --> P7[Operational Controls]
    P7 --> P8[Emergency Preparedness]
    P8 --> P9[Checking Monitoring EMS Audit]
    P9 --> P10[Management Review]
    P10 --> P11[Continual Improvement]
    P11 --> P1
    Q1[Digester H-factor] --> P3
    Q2[Effluent BOD COD] --> P3
    Q3[TRS Emissions] --> P3
    Q4[Water Consumption] --> P3
    Q5[Energy MJ per tonne] --> P3
    Q6[AOX Dioxin Levels] --> P3
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Kraft Chemical Recovery Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Wood Chips into Digester] --> R2[Pulping with White Liquor]
    R2 --> R3[Black Liquor Out]
    R3 --> R4[Evaporator Concentrator]
    R4 --> R5[Recovery Boiler Combustion]
    R5 -- Smelt --> R6[Dissolving Tank Green Liquor]
    R5 -- Steam --> R7[Turbine Generator Electricity]
    R6 --> R8[Slaker Causticizer]
    R8 --> R9[White Liquor Clarified]
    R9 --> R2
    R4 -- Condensates --> R10[Stripper]
    R10 --> R1
    R5 -- Ash --> R11[Landfill or Reuse]
    R8 -- Lime Mud --> R12[Lime Kiln]
    R12 -- Reburned Lime --> R8
```

## 4.4 Aspect-Impact Register (KTU High-Yield View)

| Aspect | Impact | Significance | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Black liquor spill | Soil & water contamination | **Critical** | Secondary containment, recovery |
| High TRS emission | Odour, health | **Major** | Recovery boiler, NCG collection |
| Bleach plant AOX | Aquatic toxicity | **Major** | Switch to ECF/TCF |
| High fresh water use | Resource depletion | **Major** | Counter-current washing, recycle |
| Sludge generation | Land burden | **Moderate** | Dewatering, co-firing |
| Energy consumption | GHG emissions | **Major** | Pinch analysis, recovery boiler |
| Bark/dust handling | Fire, dust | **Moderate** | Enclosed conveyors, sprinklers |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Kraft pulping process. List its two main advantages over the Sulphite process.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Kraft (Sulphate) pulping process** is a chemical pulping method in which wood chips are cooked in a solution of **sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium sulphide (Na₂S)** — collectively called **White Liquor** — at temperatures of **160–180 °C** for 1.5–3 hours. The active alkali attacks and dissolves lignin, liberating cellulose fibres.

**Two main advantages over the Sulphite process:**

1. **Wider raw material flexibility** — Kraft pulping tolerates nearly all wood species (resinous pines to hardwoods), whereas the Sulphite process is highly species-sensitive.
2. **Efficient chemical recovery** — the dissolved organics and inorganics leave the digester as **Black Liquor**, which is concentrated, burned in a recovery boiler, and reconverted to White Liquor — yielding both **chemical regeneration and renewable energy** (the mill becomes a net exporter of electricity).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is meant by Total Reduced Sulphur (TRS) compounds? Why are they critical in pulp mill EMS?**

**Model Answer:**

**Total Reduced Sulphur (TRS)** compounds are a group of **sulphur-containing gases** released primarily from Kraft pulp mill operations. They include:

- **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S)**
- **Methyl Mercaptan (CH₃SH)**
- **Dimethyl Sulphide (CH₃–S–CH₃)**
- **Dimethyl Disulphide (CH₃–S–S–CH₃)**

**Why critical in EMS:**

- **Olfactory nuisance**: TRS has an odour threshold as low as **0.5 ppb**, triggering community complaints.
- **Health effects**: Chronic exposure causes respiratory irritation, olfactory fatigue, and neurological effects.
- **Environmental damage**: TRS contributes to **acid rain** (via SO₂ oxidation) and tropospheric ozone formation.
- **CPCB regulatory limit**: TRS emissions must remain **below 5 ppm** (or lower as per state PCB norms) — a key **Operational Control** under ISO 14001 EMS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students frequently confuse TRS with SO₂. TRS is **reduced sulphur emitted at lower temperatures** (e.g., from digester vents, brown stock washers, evaporators), while SO₂ is the **oxidized form** from the recovery boiler stack. They have separate control technologies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete process flow of an integrated Kraft pulp and paper mill with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of the **Tomlinson Recovery Boiler** in achieving environmental sustainability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Kraft mill produces 250 tonnes/day of bleached pulp. The mill draws **45 m³ of fresh water per tonne of pulp**. The effluent has BOD₅ = **2,200 mg/L** and COD = **6,500 mg/L**. The CPCB standard is **30 kg BOD/tonne** (post-treatment). Calculate:
- (i) Total daily BOD load (kg/day)
- (ii) Required BOD removal efficiency (%)
- (iii) Whether the wastewater is biodegradable. Comment.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Process Flow and Recovery Boiler (7 Marks):**

A modern integrated Kraft mill consists of **two coupled loops** — a *fibre loop* and a *chemical recovery loop*.

**Fibre loop:**
1. **Wood yard** — debarking, chipping.
2. **Digester** — cooks chips in White Liquor at ~170 °C; lignin dissolves into Black Liquor.
3. **Brown stock washing** — multi-stage counter-current washers recover dissolved lignin.
4. **Screening/cleaning** — removes shives and grit.
5. **Oxygen delignification** — reduces Kappa number before bleaching.
6. **Bleach plant** — ECF (D₀EopD₁) or TCF sequences.
7. **Paper machine** — Fourdrinier forming, pressing, drying, calendering.

**Chemical recovery loop (role of Tomlinson Recovery Boiler):**
- Concentrated Black Liquor (~65–80% solids) is sprayed into the **Tomlinson Recovery Boiler** — a specialized combustion unit.
- **Organic portion** burns to generate **high-pressure steam** for turbines (electricity) and process heating.
- **Inorganic portion** is reduced to a molten **smelt** (Na₂CO₃ + Na₂S), which dissolves to form **Green Liquor**.
- Green Liquor is **causticized** to regenerate **White Liquor** — closing the chemical loop.

**Sustainability outcome:**
- **Energy self-sufficiency** — modern mills export surplus electricity.
- **Chemical recovery > 95%** — drastically reduces fresh chemical demand.
- **Drastic reduction of BOD/COD load** to the effluent treatment plant.

*Block diagram: refer to SECTION 4.1 above (mark awarded for neat block diagram with proper labels).*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** Production = 250 t/d, Water = 45 m³/t, BOD₅ = 2,200 mg/L, COD = 6,500 mg/L, Standard = 30 kg BOD/t.

**(i) Total daily BOD load:**

$$
Q = 250 \times 45 = 11{,}250 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}
$$

$$
\text{BOD Load} = 11{,}250 \times 2{,}200 \times 10^{-3} = 24{,}750 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**(ii) Required removal efficiency:**

$$
\text{Actual BOD/tonne} = \dfrac{24{,}750}{250} = 99 \text{ kg/t}
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{99 - 30}{99} \times 100 = 69.7\%
$$

**(iii) Biodegradability assessment:**

$$
\text{COD/BOD}_5 = \dfrac{6{,}500}{2{,}200} = 2.95
$$

A COD/BOD ratio **< 3** indicates the effluent is **readily biodegradable** — biological treatment (e.g., activated sludge, UASB) is the appropriate primary treatment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2025]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **environmental aspects and impacts** of a pulp and paper industry. Prepare an **Aspect-Impact Register** for any five key activities. Explain how ISO 14001 EMS can be applied to manage these aspects.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **ECF** and **TCF** bleaching technologies in terms of environmental performance, chemical usage, effluent characteristics, and cost. Which is more aligned with sustainable manufacturing?

#### Model Solution

**(a) Aspect-Impact Register and ISO 14001 Application (7 Marks):**

The pulp and paper industry interacts with the environment at multiple levels — air, water, soil, and biodiversity. Under **ISO 14001:2015 Clause 6.1.2 (Environmental Aspects)**, the mill must identify each activity, the aspect, the impact, and the significance.

| # | Activity | Aspect | Impact | Significance | ISO 14001 Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Digester operation | Spent liquor (Black Liquor) release | Soil, water contamination | **Critical** | Operational control procedure; secondary containment |
| 2 | Recovery boiler | TRS / SO₂ / NOₓ emission | Air pollution, acid rain | **Major** | Continuous emission monitoring (CEMS); NCG collection |
| 3 | Bleach plant | AOX, dioxins, furans | Aquatic toxicity, bioaccumulation | **Major** | Substitution to ECF/TCF; ClO₂ dosing controls |
| 4 | Effluent treatment | High BOD/COD effluent | Eutrophication, oxygen depletion | **Major** | Activated sludge + tertiary treatment |
| 5 | Wood yard | Dust, fire risk from bark piles | Air quality, fire hazard | **Moderate** | Enclosed conveyors, water sprinklers |
| 6 | Lime kiln | CO₂, particulate matter | GHG emissions, respiratory issues | **Moderate** | Cyclone separators, bag filters |
| 7 | Paper machine | Fresh water consumption | Resource depletion | **Major** | Counter-current washing, water pinch analysis |

**ISO 14001:2015 Application:**
- **Clause 4 — Context**: Identify local environmental conditions (river downstream users).
- **Clause 6.1.2 — Aspects**: Register above aspects; rank by significance.
- **Clause 6.1.3 — Compliance Obligations**: Track CPCB, State PCB, and Forest Conservation Act requirements.
- **Clause 7.5 — Documented Information**: Maintain SOPs, logbooks, calibration records.
- **Clause 9.1 — Monitoring**: CEMS, online BOD/COD analysers.
- **Clause 10 — Improvement**: Set objective of "Reduce specific water consumption by 15% in 2 years."

**(b) ECF vs. TCF Bleaching (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | **ECF (Elemental Chlorine Free)** | **TCF (Totally Chlorine Free)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Bleaching agents** | ClO₂ + O₂ + H₂O₂ + NaOH | O₃, H₂O₂, O₂, peracids (no chlorine compounds) |
| **AOX in effluent** | Low (0.2–0.5 kg Cl/t pulp) | **Negligible** (< 0.05 kg Cl/t pulp) |
| **Dioxin/furan formation** | **Very low** (modern mills below detection) | **None** |
| **Effluent toxicity** | Low | **Lowest** |
| **Pulp brightness** | Up to **90+ ISO** | Up to **88–89 ISO** (slight limitation) |
| **Strength loss** | Minimal | Slight — ozone can weaken fibres |
| **Operating cost** | **Moderate** | **Higher** (more ozone/peracid cost) |
| **Capital cost** | Moderate retrofit | Higher (ozone generator) |
| **Market acceptance** | **High** (most accepted globally) | Premium niche, eco-labelled products |

**Sustainability Verdict:**
**TCF** is theoretically the most sustainable — eliminating chlorinated organics entirely. However, **modern ECF** (with proper ClO₂ substitution) achieves effluent quality **indistinguishable from TCF** at lower cost and with brighter pulp. Hence, **ECF is the practical sweet spot** for sustainable manufacturing, while TCF is preferred for **eco-label certification** (e.g., FSC, Blue Angel, EU Ecolabel).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake**: Students often claim ECF is "harmful" because it contains "Cl" in ClO₂. The chlorine in ClO₂ behaves very differently from Cl₂ gas — it forms predominantly **chlorinated phenols (degradable)** rather than persistent dioxins. Modern ECF mills emit **< 0.1 pg TEQ/L dioxins**, well below WHO drinking-water guidelines.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Kraft (Sulphate) process** dominates global pulp production (~80%) due to fibre strength and chemical recovery.
- ✅ **Black liquor recovery** in the **Tomlinson Recovery Boiler** is the heart of pulp mill EMS — it provides both chemical regeneration and renewable energy.
- ✅ **H-Factor** = ∫ exp(43.2 − 16,113/T) dt; used universally to model delignification kinetics.
- ✅ **Active Alkali (AA)** = NaOH + ½ Na₂S (as Na₂O); **Sulphidity** = Na₂S / (NaOH + Na₂S) × 100% (optimum 25–35%).
- ✅ Water consumption = **75–250 m³/tonne**; major target for EMS-driven reduction.
- ✅ **TRS** compounds (H₂S, mercaptans) are critical air emissions — must be controlled below CPCB limits.
- ✅ **AOX, dioxins, furans** — legacy pollutants from chlorine bleaching; mitigated by **ECF** (ClO₂-based) or **TCF** (ozone/peracid) sequences.
- ✅ **COD/BOD ratio ≈ 2–3** in pulp mill effluent → readily biodegradable → activated sludge / UASB treatment is effective.
- ✅ **CPCB standard** for bleached pulp mills ≤ **25–30 kg BOD/tonne** post-treatment; specific water consumption ≤ **50 m³/tonne** for large mills.
- ✅ **ISO 14001:2015** aspects for paper mills include air emissions, water consumption, effluent quality, chemical storage, energy, and biodiversity (afforestation).
- ✅ **EIA Notification 2006** mandates Environmental Impact Assessment for new pulp and paper projects > 30,000 TPA.
- ✅ **Wood is the dominant raw material** in India (~70%), with **bagasse and waste paper** as important secondary sources.
- ✅ **Pulping methods**: Mechanical (90–95% yield, low strength), Sulphite (obsolete in India), **Kraft (best balance)**, NSSC (corrugating medium).
- ✅ **Pulp yield** (Kraft) = 45–55%; **Pulp consistency** definitions vary: 3–5% in dilute systems, 25–35% in high-density storage.
- ✅ **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** is increasingly mandated by State PCBs in India — achieved via evaporation + crystallization.
- ✅ Always remember to **convert mg/L to kg/m³** (multiply by 10⁻³) when computing pollutant loads — most common numerical error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electroplating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroplating** is an **electrochemical surface-engineering process** in which a thin, adherent layer of a desired metal (*M*) is deposited onto the surface of a conductive substrate (the *cathode*) by passing a controlled direct electric current through an electrolyte solution containing ions of that metal. From an **Environmental Management Systems (EMS)** perspective, electroplating is classified as a **highly polluting industrial process** because it generates hazardous wastewater laden with **heavy metals (Cr, Ni, Cd, Pb, Cu, Zn)**, **cyanides**, **acid/alkali effluents**, airborne acid mists, and **toxic sludge** — all of which fall under the regulatory ambit of **ISO 14001**, the **Indian Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules**, and the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.

## 1.2 Key Components of an Electroplating System

| S.No. | Component | Function | Environmental Relevance |
|:-----:|:----------|:---------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Anode** (+) | Source of metal ions (soluble) or inert electrode | Soluble anodes dissolve → replenish electrolyte |
| 2 | **Cathode** (−) | Work-piece to be plated | Uniform deposition depends on throw power |
| 3 | **Electrolyte (Plating Bath)** | Conductive ionic medium | Source of **heavy metal contamination** in effluent |
| 4 | **DC Power Source (Rectifier)** | Provides controlled DC current/voltage | Energy consumption → carbon footprint |
| 5 | **Plating Tank** | Holds electrolyte, often lined with PVC/PP | Spent bath → hazardous solid waste |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Painting With Metal Using Electricity"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are spray-painting a metal chair, but instead of using a spray gun loaded with liquid paint, you use an invisible "electric paintbrush" that pulls metal ions out of a chemical bath and glues them atom-by-atom onto the chair's surface.** The chair is connected to the **negative terminal** (it *attracts* positive metal ions), and a sacrificial metal block is connected to the **positive terminal** (it slowly dissolves to *supply* fresh metal ions into the bath). The liquid in the tank acts like a watery highway that lets the metal ions travel under the influence of electric current. While this gives a beautiful, corrosion-resistant finish, the *leftover bath water is toxic* — which is exactly why environmental management of this process is so critical.

## 1.4 Why Electroplating Matters in an EMS Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard industrial highlight (KTU Board Favourite):** The electroplating industry is among the **17 categories of "Highly Polluting Industries"** identified by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India**. Any unit engaged in electroplating **must** obtain **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** under the **Water Act, 1974** and **Air Act, 1981**, and is required to install an **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** with provisions for **metal recovery, sludge management, and zero liquid discharge (ZLD)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Ionization at the Anode (Oxidation):**
   The metal anode dissolves into the electrolyte by losing electrons:
   $$M_{(s)} \longrightarrow M^{n+}_{(aq)} + ne^{-}$$
2. **Migration Under Electric Field:**
   The positively charged metal cations ($M^{n+}$) migrate through the electrolyte toward the **negatively charged cathode** (work-piece).
3. **Reduction at the Cathode:**
   At the cathode surface, the metal ions gain electrons and deposit as a thin metallic film:
   $$M^{n+}_{(aq)} + ne^{-} \longrightarrow M_{(s)}$$
4. **Concurrent Side Reactions:**
   - Hydrogen evolution at cathode: $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}\uparrow$
   - Oxygen evolution at anode (for inert anodes): $2H_{2}O \rightarrow O_{2}\uparrow + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-}$

## 2.2 Process Parameters Affecting Plating Quality

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Deposit | EMS Consideration |
|:----------|:-------------:|:-----------------|:------------------|
| **Current Density** | 1 – 50 A/dm² | Higher → finer grain, but may cause burning | Higher current → more H₂ → more mist |
| **Bath Temperature** | 25 – 65 °C | Affects ion mobility | Energy use, fume generation |
| **pH of Bath** | 0.5 – 14 | Influences deposit brightness & adhesion | Acid/alkali waste |
| **Agitation** | Mechanical / air | Prevents concentration polarization | Air agitation → toxic aerosol |
| **Bath Composition** | Metal salt + additives | Brightness, hardness, leveling | Spent bath = hazardous waste |

## 2.3 Environmental Pollutants Generated

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** Always remember the **Big-5 toxic heavy metals** in plating effluent — **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺), Nickel (Ni²⁺), Cadmium (Cd²⁺), Lead (Pb²⁺), Copper (Cu²⁺)** — alongside **cyanide (CN⁻)**, **acids (H₂SO₄, HCl)**, and **alkalis (NaOH)**.

### 2.3.1 Wastewater (Liquid Effluent)

- **Process rinse water** — the largest volume of wastewater.
- **Spent plating baths** — extremely concentrated, treated as hazardous.
- Contains: free cyanide, complexed cyanides, hexavalent chromium, dissolved heavy metals.

### 2.3.2 Air Emissions

- **Chromic acid mist** (CrO₃ droplets) — from hard-chrome plating.
- **Hydrogen cyanide (HCN) gas** — from cyanide baths at low pH.
- **HCl fumes, NOₓ, acid mists.**
- Controlled via **ventilation, demisters, scrubbers**.

### 2.3.3 Solid / Hazardous Waste

- **Metal hydroxide sludge** from precipitation.
- **Spent anodes, filter cartridges, contaminated PPE.**
- Classified under **Schedule-II / Schedule-III of Hazardous Waste Rules, 2016 (India)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` (not the bare pipe character) in formulas to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| S.No. | Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|:-----:|:--------|:-------------------|:--------------|:------|
| 1 | **Faraday's First Law** | $m \propto Q$ | $m$ = mass deposited; $Q$ = charge | g ; C |
| 2 | **Faraday's Second Law** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $I$ = current; $t$ = time; $M$ = molar mass; $n$ = valency; $F$ = Faraday constant | $F = \mathbf{96\,485\ C\,mol^{-1}}$ |
| 3 | **Cathodic Current Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ = efficiency | % |
| 4 | **Theoretical Mass via Faraday** | $m_{th} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Theoretical deposit mass | g |
| 5 | **Equivalent Weight** | $E = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Molar mass per electron | g/eq |
| 6 | **Mass using Electrochemical Equivalent** | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | $Z$ = ECE = $E/F$ | g |
| 7 | **Required Current for Target Mass** | $I = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{t \cdot M}$ | — | A |
| 8 | **Concentration from Dilution** | $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ | For rinse-water mixing | mg/L · L |
| 9 | **Removal Efficiency** | $\%R = \dfrac{C_i - C_f}{C_i} \times 100$ | $C_i$ inlet, $C_f$ outlet | % |
| 10 | **Sludge Volume** | $V_s = \dfrac{m_{metal}}{\rho_{sludge}}$ | Mass of metal in sludge | m³ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry** — chrome plating on bumpers, fuel-injection parts.
- **Electronics** — gold/silver plating on PCBs, connectors.
- **Aerospace & Defence** — cadmium plating for corrosion resistance on landing gear.
- **EMS Linkage** — drives **Cleaner Production** audits, **Life-Cycle Assessment (LCA)**, **Hazardous Waste Manifest** tracking, and **ISO 14001 EMS** documentation for environmental aspects & impacts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

### 3.1.1 Starting Principles

We begin from the fundamental relationship: **1 mole of electrons carries 1 Faraday of charge**, where $F = 96\,485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$ (often rounded to $96\,500\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$).

The charge $Q$ passed through the circuit is given by:

$$
Q = I \cdot t
$$

where:
- $I$ is the current in **amperes (A)**
- $t$ is the time in **seconds (s)**
- $Q$ is the total charge in **coulombs (C)**

### 3.1.2 Number of Faradays Passed

The number of Faradays of charge transferred is:

$$
n_{F} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}
$$

This represents the **moles of electrons** that have moved through the external circuit.

### 3.1.3 Moles of Metal Deposited

For a metal ion $M^{n+}$, reduction at the cathode requires **$n$ electrons per ion**:

$$
M^{n+} + ne^{-} \longrightarrow M_{(s)}
$$

Therefore, **1 Faraday** of charge deposits **$\dfrac{1}{n}$ moles** of metal.

Hence, the moles of metal actually deposited at the cathode are:

$$
n_{mol} = \dfrac{n_{F}}{n} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

### 3.1.4 Mass of Metal Deposited

If $M$ is the **molar mass** (atomic weight) of the metal in $\text{g mol}^{-1}$, then the mass deposited is:

$$
m = n_{mol} \cdot M
$$

Substituting:

$$
m = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \cdot M
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Consolidated Equation (Faraday's Law of Electrolysis):**
>
> $$\boxed{\,m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}\,}$$
>
> This is the **single most important equation** for the topic *Electroplating* in the KTU 2024 EMS syllabus.

### 3.1.5 Electrochemical Equivalent (ECE)

Define:

$$
Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{E}{F}
$$

Then the equation simplifies to:

$$
m = Z \cdot I \cdot t
$$

The units of $Z$ are **grams per ampere-second** (g/C) or **g/(A·h)** with $1\ \text{A·h} = 3600\ \text{C}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Mass Calculation

> **Problem:** A nickel-plating bath operates at a current of **15 A** for **2.5 hours**. Given $M_{Ni} = 58.7\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$ and $n = 2$, calculate the theoretical mass of nickel deposited.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert time to seconds:

$$
t = 2.5\ \text{h} \times 3600\ \text{s/h} = 9000\ \text{s}
$$

Step 2 — Compute total charge:

$$
Q = I \cdot t = 15 \times 9000 = 135\,000\ \text{C}
$$

Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law:

$$
m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{15 \times 9000 \times 58.7}{2 \times 96\,485}
$$

Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 15 \times 9000 \times 58.7 = 7\,924\,500
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 96\,485 = 192\,970
$$

Step 5 — Final division:

$$
m = \dfrac{7\,924\,500}{192\,970} = 41.07\ \text{g}
$$

> **Answer:** Theoretical deposit ≈ **41.07 g of nickel**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Cathodic Current Efficiency

> **Problem:** In an actual run, the mass deposited was found to be **35.0 g** instead of the theoretical 41.07 g. Calculate the **cathodic current efficiency**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Recall the efficiency formula:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100
$$

Step 2 — Substitute:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{35.0}{41.07} \times 100
$$

Step 3 — Compute:

$$
\eta = 0.8522 \times 100 = 85.22\ \%
$$

> **Answer:** $\eta \approx \mathbf{85.2\ \%}$. The remaining $\sim 14.8\%$ of the current went into parasitic side reactions, mainly **hydrogen evolution**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Time Required for Target Mass

> **Problem:** How long must a current of **20 A** be passed through a **copper sulphate bath ($Cu^{2+}$)** to deposit **50 g** of copper? Given $M_{Cu} = 63.5\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$, $n = 2$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Rearrange Faraday's law to isolate $t$:

$$
t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute values:

$$
t = \dfrac{50 \times 2 \times 96\,485}{20 \times 63.5}
$$

Step 3 — Numerator and denominator:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 50 \times 2 \times 96\,485 = 9\,648\,500
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 20 \times 63.5 = 1270
$$

Step 4 — Division:

$$
t = \dfrac{9\,648\,500}{1270} = 7598.8\ \text{s}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to hours:

$$
t = \dfrac{7598.8}{3600} = 2.11\ \text{h}
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **2.11 hours** are required.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pollution & Control Mapping

| Pollutant Stream | Source | Health Impact | Treatment Technology |
|:-----------------|:-------|:--------------|:--------------------|
| Cr⁶⁺ (Hexavalent) | Hard chrome plating | Carcinogen, lung damage | Reduction to Cr³⁺ (FeSO₄/SO₂) → precipitation |
| CN⁻ (Free cyanide) | Cu/Zn/Ag cyanide baths | Lethal; blocks cytochrome oxidase | Alkaline chlorination, ozone oxidation |
| Ni²⁺ | Decorative plating | Dermatitis, carcinogen | Hydroxide precipitation, ion exchange |
| Cd²⁺ | Cadmium plating | Kidney damage, Itai-Itai disease | Hydroxide precipitation, RO |
| Pb²⁺ | Pb-Sn alloy plating | Neurotoxin | Hydroxide/sulfide precipitation |
| Acid mists | HCl, H₂SO₄ baths | Respiratory irritation | Demister + alkaline scrubber |
| Metal sludge | Precipitation stage | Leachate hazard | Stabilization, secure landfilling |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electroplating Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Rectifier Power Supply] -->|Positive Lead| B(Anode - Sacrificial Metal Block)
    A -->|Negative Lead| C(Cathode - Work Piece to be Plated)
    B -->|Oxidation M → Mn+ + ne-| D(Plating Bath Electrolyte Tank)
    C -->|Reduction Mn+ + ne- → M| D
    D -->|Acid Mist and Fumes| E[Exhaust Hood and Demister Unit]
    D -->|Rinse Water Dragout| F[Effluent Treatment Plant ETP]
    F -->|Precipitation pH 8 to 9| G[Metal Hydroxide Sludge]
    F -->|Clarified Water| H[Recycle or ZLD System]
    G -->|Secure Landfill| I[Hazardous Waste Disposal Site]
    E -->|Alkaline Scrubber| J[Clean Air to Atmosphere]
    H --> K[Zero Liquid Discharge]
```

## 4.2 Electroplating Effluent Treatment Flow (Function-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plating Rinse Water] --> Q1[Equalization and Cyanide Destruction Tank]
    Q1 --> Q2[Hexavalent Chromium Reduction Tank]
    Q2 --> Q3[pH Adjustment and Coagulation Tank]
    Q3 --> Q4[Lamella Clarifier or Settling Tank]
    Q4 --> Q5[Sand Filter and Activated Carbon Polisher]
    Q5 --> Q6[Reverse Osmosis or Ion Exchange]
    Q6 --> R1[Treated Water Recycle]
    Q4 --> S1[Filter Press for Sludge Dewatering]
    S1 --> S2[Cake to TSDF Secured Landfill]
    Q1 --> A1[Alkaline Chlorination for CN-]
    Q2 --> A2[FeSO4 or SO2 for Cr6+ to Cr3+]
```

## 4.3 EMS Integration Schematic for an Electroplating Unit

```mermaid

    O1[ISO 14001 Environmental Policy] --> O2[Environmental Aspects Register]
    O2 --> O3[Identification of Significant Aspects]

    O4 --> O5[Objectives Targets and Programmes OTPs]
    O5 --> O6[Operational Controls - ETP, Scrubber, SOPs]
    O6 --> O7[Monitoring and Measurement]
    O7 --> O8[Corrective and Preventive Actions CAPA]
    O8 --> O9[Management Review]
    O9 --> O1
    O2 --> N1[Heavy Metal Effluent]
    O2 --> N2[Cyanide Waste]
    O2 --> N3[Acid Mist Emissions]
    O2 --> N4[Sludge Disposal]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define electroplating. List any four heavy metals commonly used in electroplating baths.**

**Model Answer:**

Electroplating is an electrochemical process in which a metal coating is deposited onto a conductive substrate by passing direct current through an electrolyte containing ions of the plating metal.

**Four commonly used heavy metals:**
1. Chromium (Cr)
2. Nickel (Ni)
3. Copper (Cu)
4. Zinc (Zn) / Cadmium (Cd) *(any four)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte: $m \propto Q$.

> **Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their **equivalent weights**: $m \propto E$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Internal choice is mandatory.* Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as two independent alternatives.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the **electrochemical principle of electroplating** with a neat labelled diagram. Describe the role of the anode, cathode, and electrolyte. Identify the **major environmental pollutants** generated and their **sources**.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A chrome plating bath operates at **30 A** for **4 hours**. Calculate the theoretical mass of chromium deposited. Given $M_{Cr} = 52\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$, $n = 3$, $F = 96\,500\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$. If the actual deposit is **38 g**, determine the **cathodic current efficiency**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle:**
Electroplating is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. When DC current flows through an electrolyte, **oxidation** occurs at the anode (metal dissolves to give $M^{n+}$) and **reduction** occurs at the cathode (metal ions deposit on the work-piece).

**Roles:**
- **Anode:** Soluble anode of the plating metal dissolves to replenish ions.
- **Cathode:** The work-piece to be plated; site of metal deposition.
- **Electrolyte:** Provides metal ions and ensures ionic conductivity.

**Environmental Pollutants & Sources:**
- *Hexavalent Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)* — from chrome plating bath; carcinogenic.
- *Nickel, Cadmium, Lead, Copper ions* — from rinse water dragout.
- *Free Cyanide (CN⁻)* — from cyanide-complex baths.
- *Acid/alkali effluents* — from spent baths and rinses.
- *Acid mist* — from CrO₃ baths.
- *Metal hydroxide sludge* — from ETP.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute time in seconds.

$$
t = 4 \times 3600 = 14\,400\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Faraday's Law.

$$
m_{th} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{30 \times 14\,400 \times 52}{3 \times 96\,500}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator.

$$
30 \times 14\,400 \times 52 = 22\,464\,000
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator.

$$
3 \times 96\,500 = 289\,500
$$

**Step 5:** Final mass.

$$
m_{th} = \dfrac{22\,464\,000}{289\,500} = 77.59\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 6:** Cathodic current efficiency.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100 = \dfrac{38}{77.59} \times 100 = 48.97\ \%
$$

> **Final Answer:** Theoretical mass ≈ **77.59 g**; Current efficiency ≈ **48.97 %**.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the **environmental impacts** of electroplating operations and explain the **commonly adopted effluent treatment scheme** for a chromium-bearing plating wastewater.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A nickel-plating bath uses a current of **12 A** for **3 hours**. Given $M_{Ni} = 58.7\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96\,500\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$, calculate:
   - (i) The theoretical mass of nickel deposited.
   - (ii) The time required to deposit **80 g** of nickel if the current is increased to **25 A**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Environmental Impacts:**
- Soil and groundwater contamination due to seepage of untreated effluent containing Cr, Ni, Cd.
- Surface water toxicity to aquatic life.
- Air pollution from Cr acid mist and cyanide fumes.
- Worker health hazards — dermatitis, lung cancer, neurological disorders.
- Generation of large volumes of toxic metal sludge.

**Effluent Treatment Scheme for Cr-bearing Wastewater:**

1. **Equalization** — homogenize flow and concentration.
2. **Hexavalent Chromium Reduction** — reduce Cr⁶⁺ to Cr³⁺ using **FeSO₄** (at pH < 3) or **SO₂** gas.
   $$Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-} + 6Fe^{2+} + 14H^{+} \longrightarrow 2Cr^{3+} + 6Fe^{3+} + 7H_{2}O$$
3. **pH Adjustment** — raise pH to 8–9 with lime/NaOH to precipitate metal hydroxides.
   $$Cr^{3+} + 3OH^{-} \longrightarrow Cr(OH)_{3}\downarrow$$
4. **Coagulation–Flocculation–Sedimentation** — settle out the sludge.
5. **Filtration & Polishing** — sand filter + activated carbon.
6. **Sludge Dewatering** — filter press; cake sent to **TSDF**.
7. **Treated Water** — recycled or sent to **ZLD** (multiple-effect evaporator + crystallizer).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Part (i): Theoretical mass**

**Step 1:** Time in seconds.

$$
t = 3 \times 3600 = 10\,800\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Faraday's Law.

$$
m_{th} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{12 \times 10\,800 \times 58.7}{2 \times 96\,500}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator.

$$
12 \times 10\,800 \times 58.7 = 7\,607\,520
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator.

$$
2 \times 96\,500 = 193\,000
$$

**Step 5:** Final mass.

$$
m_{th} = \dfrac{7\,607\,520}{193\,000} = 39.42\ \text{g}
$$

**Part (ii): Time for 80 g at 25 A**

**Step 1:** Rearrange Faraday's Law.

$$
t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
t = \dfrac{80 \times 2 \times 96\,500}{25 \times 58.7}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator.

$$
80 \times 2 \times 96\,500 = 15\,440\,000
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator.

$$
25 \times 58.7 = 1467.5
$$

**Step 5:** Time in seconds.

$$
t = \dfrac{15\,440\,000}{1467.5} = 10\,522.6\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to hours.

$$
t = \dfrac{10\,522.6}{3600} = 2.92\ \text{h}
$$

> **Final Answer:** (i) Theoretical mass ≈ **39.42 g**; (ii) Time required ≈ **2.92 hours**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU exams on *Electroplating*:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Forgetting to convert *hours → seconds* in Faraday's Law (results in a mass answer that is off by a factor of **3600**). Always show the conversion step.
> 2. **Valency Confusion:** Using the wrong value of **$n$**. For chromium plating use **$n = 3$** (Cr³⁺ → Cr), for chromium from chromic acid the reduction is **$n = 6$** (CrO₃ → Cr). Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Efficiency Direction:** Cathodic current efficiency **always = actual / theoretical × 100**, never the reverse.
> 4. **Missing EMS Context:** Simply writing chemistry without linking to **ISO 14001 aspects and impacts** loses 2–3 marks in Part-B (a) sub-questions. Always mention **regulatory framework + pollutants + control measures**.
> 5. **Schematic Quality:** KTU expects **labelled block diagrams** showing at least: rectifier → bath → anode/cathode → effluent. A blank line labelled "diagram" costs marks.
> 6. **Sign of electrode:** KTU repeatedly tests whether students remember that the **work-piece is the CATHODE (negative)**. Confusing it costs marks.
> 7. **Cr⁶⁺ Reduction Step:** In treatment questions, mention the **reducing agent AND pH condition**; partial answers get partial marks only.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electroplating (Module 3)**

- ⭐ Electroplating = **electrochemical deposition** of a metal on a conductive cathode using DC current.
- ⭐ Master equation: **$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$**, where $F = \mathbf{96\,485\ C\,mol^{-1}}$.
- ⭐ **Cathode = work-piece (−)**, **Anode = plating metal (+)**, **Electrolyte = metal-salt solution**.
- ⭐ The **Big-5 hazardous metals**: Cr, Ni, Cd, Pb, Cu.
- ⭐ **Cyanide baths** → free CN⁻ → controlled by **alkaline chlorination** ($CN^{-} + 2OH^{-} + Cl_{2} \rightarrow CO_{3}^{2-} + 2Cl^{-} + N_{2} + H_{2}O$).
- ⭐ **Hexavalent chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** is reduced to **Cr³⁺** using **FeSO₄ / SO₂** at **pH < 3**, then precipitated as **Cr(OH)₃** at **pH 8–9**.
- ⭐ **Current efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ — usually **70–90 %** for nickel, lower for chromium.
- ⭐ **Electrochemical Equivalent (ECE)** $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ — units **g/(A·s)** or **g/(A·h)**.
- ⭐ **Indian EMS Regulatory Hooks:** Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, CPCB "17 highly polluting industries" list.
- ⭐ **Global EMS Hook:** ISO 14001:2015 mandates **identification of environmental aspects**, **legal compliance evaluation**, and **operational control** for plating operations.
- ⭐ **Air Emissions:** CrO₃ mist (from hard-chrome); HCN gas (from cyanide baths at low pH).
- ⭐ **Solid Waste:** Metal hydroxide sludge → TSDF / secure landfill.
- ⭐ **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** is increasingly mandated for new plating units in India.
- ⭐ For **KTU numericals**: always show **time in seconds**, use $F = 96\,500\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$, round answer to **2 decimal places**.
- ⭐ Remember **parallel side reaction**: hydrogen evolution at cathode reduces plating efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tanning Industry
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tanning Industry

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tanning Industry (Leather Processing Industry):** The tanning industry refers to the sector of manufacturing that converts raw animal hides and skins (putrescible biological tissue) into durable, non-putrescible, and commercially valuable **leather** through a sequence of chemical and mechanical unit operations collectively termed *tanning*. It is formally classified under **RED category** of industries by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India, signifying that it generates highly polluting effluents and emissions, and operates under stringent environmental compliance regulations.

From an **Environment Management Systems (EMS)** perspective, the tanning industry is treated as a *case study* of a **resource-intensive, high-pollution-load manufacturing process** used by KTU to illustrate the practical application of:
- **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**
- **Cleaner Production (CP)**
- **End-of-Pipe Treatment Technologies**
- **Hazardous Waste Management Rules (India)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pickle, the Veggie, and the Chrome":**
> Imagine a fresh piece of meat left in open air — within hours it rots, smells terrible, and attracts flies. Now imagine *cooking* that meat with salt, spices, and heat. It lasts for months. **Tanning is essentially "cooking" animal skin at a chemical level.** The raw hide is the perishable "meat," and the tanning agent (vegetable tannins, chromium salts, alum, or synthetic agents) acts like the "spice" that permanently locks the protein fibers in place, transforming a rotting biological material into a stable, durable, flexible, and commercially valuable product — leather.

A typical hide contains roughly **60–65% water**, **30–35% protein** (chiefly **collagen**), and **2–5% fats/non-collagenous matter**. The tanning process chemically stabilizes the collagen fiber matrix to prevent putrefaction.

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Focus:**
> The official Module 3 of OEBBT831 (Environmental Management Systems) covers industrial case studies including **Tanning Industry, Pulp & Paper, and Textile Industry** with emphasis on:
> - Process flow & unit operations
> - Sources and characteristics of pollution
> - Wastewater characterization (BOD, COD, TDS, SS, Chromium, Sulfide, Nitrogen)
> - Treatment technologies (Primary → Secondary → Tertiary)
> - Cleaner production opportunities

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Wastewater generated | **28–35 L/kg of raw hide processed** |
| pH of combined effluent | **8.0 – 11.0 (highly alkaline)** |
| BOD₅ of raw effluent | **2,000 – 5,000 mg/L** |
| COD of raw effluent | **5,000 – 12,000 mg/L** |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | **2,000 – 8,000 mg/L** |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | **10,000 – 30,000 mg/L** |
| Total Chromium (Cr³⁺ + Cr⁶⁺) | **50 – 500 mg/L** |
| Sulfide (S²⁻) | **200 – 1,500 mg/L** |
| Chloride (Cl⁻) | **5,000 – 20,000 mg/L** |
| Sodium (Na⁺) | **2,000 – 8,000 mg/L** |
| Nitrogen (TKN) | **200 – 1,200 mg/L** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Flow — Step-by-Step Logic

The tanning process is divided into **four broad stages** that progressively transform raw hide into finished leather. Each stage is a **distinct unit operation** with its own chemical inputs and waste streams.

### **Stage 1: Pre-Tanning (Beamhouse Operations)**

The beamhouse prepares the raw hide for tanning by removing unwanted materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pre-Tanning?**
> Raw hide contains hair, epidermis, flesh, fat, soluble proteins, and dirt. These must be removed because they (i) interfere with tanning chemical penetration, (ii) cause uneven leather quality, and (iii) generate highly polluting waste streams.

| Sub-Step | Chemical / Method | Purpose | Waste Generated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soaking | Water + wetting agents + bactericides (e.g., Na₂CO₃) | Rehydrate salted/cured hides, remove dirt & blood | Soak liquor (high BOD, salt, blood) |
| Liming | Ca(OH)₂ + Na₂S (lime-sulfide) | Unhairing, swelling, opening fiber structure | Lime-sulfide liquor (high pH ~12, sulfide, BOD, COD) |
| Fleshing | Mechanical scraping | Remove subcutaneous flesh | Solid fleshings (biodegradable) |
| De-liming | (NH₄)₂SO₄, NH₄Cl, or weak acids | Reduce pH from 12 to 8–9 | De-liming liquor (high ammonia) |
| Bating | Proteolytic enzymes (trypsin, pancreatin) | Degrade residual proteins, soften hide | Bating liquor (high BOD, nitrogen) |
| Pickling | H₂SO₄ + NaCl | Lower pH to 2.5–3.5 for chrome tanning | Pickle liquor (high TDS, sulfate, chloride) |

### **Stage 2: Tanning (Actual Stabilization)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tanning is the irreversible chemical transformation** of putrescible hide into stable leather. The three principal tanning methods are:

#### **A. Vegetable Tanning**
- Uses natural **tannins** (polyphenols) from bark, wood, leaves (e.g., *mimosa*, *quebracho*, *chestnut*).
- Slow process (takes **weeks**).
- Produces firm, brown leather used for **soles, belts, saddlery**.
- pH range: **4.5 – 5.5** (acidic).
- Generates **Vegetable Tanning Effluent (VTE)** with high COD, color, tannins, polyphenols.

#### **B. Chrome Tanning (Mineral Tanning) — Most Common Worldwide**
- Uses **basic chromium sulfate — Cr(OH)SO₄** (commonly called "chrome liquor").
- Fast process (takes **hours**).
- Produces soft, supple, blue ("wet blue") leather.
- pH range: **2.5 – 4.0**.
- The chromium forms **coordinate covalent bonds** with collagen carboxyl groups, crosslinking the fibers.
- Generates chrome-laden wastewater — a major environmental concern.

#### **C. Other Tanning Methods**
- **Alum Tanning** (Al₂(SO₄)₃ + NaCl) — for white leather (gloves).
- **Oil Tanning** (cod liver oil) — for chamois leather.
- **Synthetic Tanning** (syntans — aromatic sulfone-condensate resins).
- **Aldehyde Tanning** (glutaraldehyde, formaldehyde) — for washable leathers.

### **Stage 3: Post-Tanning (Wet-End Operations)**

Performed after tanning to impart specific physical and chemical properties.

| Sub-Step | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Neutralization | Adjust pH to 4.5–6.5 using NaHCO₃ or Na₂CO₃ |
| Retanning | Add additional tannins (syntans, vegetable extracts) to modify feel |
| Dyeing | Apply acid, direct, or reactive dyes for color |
| Fatliquoring | Emulsified oils penetrate fibers to provide softness, flexibility, water resistance |
| Fixing | Acidify with formic acid to fix dyes/fatliquors onto leather |

### **Stage 4: Finishing (Mechanical + Surface Treatments)**

- **Sammying** (squeezing out water)
- **Setting / Drying** (vacuum drying, toggle drying, paste drying)
- **Conditioning** (controlled humidity equilibration)
- **Staking / Softening** (mechanical)
- **Buffing** (sanding surface)
- **Spray Finishing** (pigment + binder + lacquer for color, gloss, protection)
- Generates **VOC emissions** (Volatile Organic Compounds) from finishing solvents.

## 2.2 Sources of Pollution — The "Why" Behind Environmental Concern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tanning industry is one of the **top 5 most polluting industries** globally (World Bank & UNEP classification). Pollution arises from **chemical inputs** and **molecular structural changes** to the hide.

| Pollution Type | Major Pollutants | Source Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquid Wastewater** | BOD, COD, TSS, TDS, Sulfide, Chromium, Ammonia, Chloride, Color, Tannins | All stages — especially beamhouse & tanning |
| **Solid Waste** | Fleshings, trimmings, hair, buffing dust, chrome shavings, sludge | Mechanical & chemical processing |
| **Air Emissions** | H₂S (rotten-egg smell), NH₃, VOCs, PM (particulate matter) | Lime yard, spray finishing |
| **Odor** | Mercaptans, amines, indoles, skatoles | Beamhouse & sludge storage |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for solving wastewater characterization and treatment problems in KTU examinations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_i \times Q_i = C_f \times Q_f$ | Mass balance for mixing of streams | mg/L × L/d = mg/d |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency of a treatment unit | % |
| 3 | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{DO_{initial} - DO_{5day}}{Dilution\ Factor}$ | Standard 5-day BOD measurement | mg/L |
| 4 | $COD/BOD$ ratio | Biodegradability indicator — **> 5 = poorly biodegradable** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F/M = \dfrac{S_0 \times Q}{V \times X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio (Activated Sludge Design) | day⁻¹ |
| 6 | $Cr^{6+}_{toxic} \to Cr^{3+}_{less\ toxic}$ | Reduction under pH 2 with FeSO₄ or Na₂S₂O₅ | — |
| 7 | $H_2S \xrightarrow{pH > 9} HS^- \xrightarrow{pH > 12} S^{2-}$ | Sulfide speciation diagram (Henderson-Hasselbalch) | pKa ≈ 7.0, 14.0 |
| 8 | Sludge Volume Index $SVI = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{MLSS}$ | Sludge settleability in clarifier | mL/g |
| 9 | Chromium Recovery: $Cr(OH)_3 \xrightarrow{NaOH} NaCrO_2 \xrightarrow{H_2SO_4} Cr(OH)SO_4$ | Recycle loop for chrome tanning | — |
| 10 | Mass load $= C \times Q \times 10^{-3}$ | Daily pollutant loading in kg/d | kg/d |

**Boundary Conditions & Critical pH Values to Memorize:**

- **Lime-sulfide unhairing:** pH > 12 (excess lime)
- **Chrome tanning optimum:** pH **2.5 – 3.5**
- **Chromium precipitation as Cr(OH)₃:** pH **8.0 – 9.0**
- **H₂S ionization equilibria:** pKa₁ = **7.04**, pKa₂ = **12.92**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These equations are used to (i) design equalization tanks, (ii) size biological reactors, (iii) compute chrome recovery economics, and (iv) prepare **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** reports for the Tamil Nadu, West Bengal, and Uttar Pradesh tannery clusters (Kanpur, Ranipet, Ambur).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mixing Equation for Combined Tannery Effluent

Tanneries generate **multiple segregated streams** (beamhouse, chrome tanning, vegetable tanning, post-tanning) with different chemistries. These streams are usually combined in an equalization tank before treatment. The KTU problem statement typically asks: *"Given flow rates and concentrations of N streams, find the combined effluent concentration."*

### **Setup**

Consider **N = 3** streams entering a common equalization tank:

- Stream 1 (Beamhouse): $Q_1$ L/min, $C_1$ mg/L (of a pollutant, say Sulfide)
- Stream 2 (Chrome tanning): $Q_2$ L/min, $C_2$ mg/L
- Stream 3 (Post-tanning): $Q_3$ L/min, $C_3$ mg/L

**Total mass in = Total mass out** (steady-state conservation of mass).

$$M_{in} = C_1 Q_1 + C_2 Q_2 + C_3 Q_3$$

$$M_{out} = C_{mix} \times Q_{mix}$$

where $Q_{mix} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$.

### **Algebraic Derivation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{By conservation of mass:} \quad C_1 Q_1 + C_2 Q_2 + C_3 Q_3 &= C_{mix} (Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Therefore:} \quad C_{mix} &= \frac{C_1 Q_1 + C_2 Q_2 + C_3 Q_3}{Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Generalized form for N streams:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{mix} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i Q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} Q_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:**
> This **flow-weighted average** is the foundation of all wastewater design calculations. Without knowing the mixed concentration, one cannot size aeration tanks, chemical dosing units, or chromium recovery systems.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pollutant Origin vs. Treatment Strategy

| Pollutant | Origin Stage | Form in Effluent | Primary Treatment | Secondary / Tertiary Treatment | Recovery Option |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sulfide (S²⁻)** | Liming-unhairing | Na₂S, H₂S, HS⁻ | Oxidation with MnO₂ or H₂O₂ (Catalytic); Precipitation with FeSO₄ | Biological oxidation (Thiobacillus) | — |
| **Chromium (Cr³⁺)** | Chrome tanning | Basic Cr sulfate, Cr(OH)SO₄ | **Direct precipitation** at pH 8.0–9.0 as Cr(OH)₃ | Reduction of Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ (if any) using Na₂S₂O₅ | **Chrome recovery & recycle** (re-use 90–95%) |
| **High BOD/COD** | Soaking, bating, degreasing | Dissolved proteins, fats, blood | Primary sedimentation, DAF | **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**, UASB, SBR | Biogas from anaerobic digestion |
| **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | Pickling, chrome | NaCl, Na₂SO₄ | — | **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**, Evaporation | Salt recovery, water reuse |
| **Color** | Dyeing, vegetable tanning | Dyestuffs, polyphenols | Coagulation-flocculation | Ozonation, activated carbon adsorption | — |
| **Ammonia-N** | De-liming, bating | NH₄⁺, NH₃ | Air stripping at pH > 11 | Nitrification-Denitrification (N/DN) | — |
| **Oil & Grease** | Degreasing, fatliquoring | Emulsified oils | Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) with poly-aluminum chloride | — | Fat recovery for soap/feed |
| **Hair & Fleshings** | Unhairing, fleshing | Solid proteins | Screening, dissolved air flotation | Anaerobic digestion | Rendering into meat-and-bone meal |

## 3.3 Sample Numerical (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A tannery generates three wastewater streams:

- **Stream A (Beamhouse):** $Q_A = 100$ m³/d, Sulfide concentration $C_A = 800$ mg/L
- **Stream B (Chrome Tanning):** $Q_B = 50$ m³/d, Sulfide concentration $C_B = 100$ mg/L
- **Stream C (Post-tanning):** $Q_C = 80$ m³/d, Sulfide concentration $C_C = 50$ mg/L

**Find:** (a) Total sulfide mass loading in **kg/d**.
(b) Combined mixed sulfide concentration in **mg/L**.

**Solution (Step-by-step, exhaustive):**

**Part (a): Total Mass Loading**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= M_A + M_B + M_C
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_A &= C_A \times Q_A \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 800 \times 100 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 80 \text{ kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_B &= C_B \times Q_B \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 100 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 5 \text{ kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_C &= C_C \times Q_C \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 50 \times 80 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 4 \text{ kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= 80 + 5 + 4 = 89 \text{ kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b): Mixed Concentration**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_A + Q_B + Q_C \\
          &= 100 + 50 + 80 \\
          &= 230 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{mix} &= \frac{C_A Q_A + C_B Q_B + C_C Q_C}{Q_A + Q_B + Q_C}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{mix} &= \frac{(800 \times 100) + (100 \times 50) + (50 \times 80)}{230}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{mix} &= \frac{80{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 4{,}000}{230}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{mix} &= \frac{89{,}000}{230}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{mix} &\approx 386.96 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-verification using mass balance:**
> $C_{mix} \times Q_{total} \times 10^{-3} = 386.96 \times 230 \times 10^{-3} = 89.0$ kg/d ✓ — matches Part (a).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** "Each module shall have an internal choice; the student shall answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks."
